shortDescription: Surgery that lifts and repositions deeper facial tissue to soften sagging along the cheeks, jawline, and neck.

sections.overview:
A facelift is surgery that lifts and repositions the deeper tissues of the lower face and neck to soften sagging cheeks, jowls, and folds, restoring a firmer, more rested contour. Patients travel to Korea because Korean surgeons perform a high volume of facial surgery and emphasize work on the deeper support layer rather than skin alone, which tends to produce a natural result that lasts rather than a tight, pulled appearance.

The procedure addresses changes that creams and non-surgical treatments cannot fully correct, such as a heavy jowl, a sagging midface, and loose tissue under the chin. It does not stop aging, but it resets the face to a fresher version of itself, and results are designed to look like you rather than a different person.

Modern facelifts work on the SMAS, the fibrous layer beneath the skin that gives the face its support. A SMAS or deep-plane technique lifts and secures this layer, then redrapes the skin without excess tension. Depending on your needs, a neck lift can be combined to tighten loose tissue and muscle bands under the chin, and fat grafting can restore volume to areas that have hollowed with age.

Because this is a more involved operation, it is performed under general anesthesia, small drains are often placed to prevent fluid collecting, and most patients spend about one night as an inpatient for monitoring. The stated stay of about 10 days allows for consultation, surgery, the inpatient night, and the early healing and suture removal phase before international patients fly home.
sections.candidacy:
Good candidates are adults in reasonable general health who have noticeable sagging of the lower face, jowls, or neck, who do not smoke or are willing to stop well before and after surgery, and who hold realistic expectations about a natural rejuvenation rather than a complete transformation. Skin that retains some elasticity tends to redrape well, which supports a smooth result.

A facelift is not advisable for people with uncontrolled high blood pressure, bleeding or clotting disorders, unstable heart or other chronic disease, or active infection in the area, because these raise the risk of bleeding, poor healing, and anesthesia complications. Smoking is a particular concern, as it impairs blood supply to the lifted skin and can lead to healing problems, so surgeons typically require stopping for several weeks.

Patients whose main concern is fine surface wrinkles or skin texture rather than sagging may be better served by non-surgical treatments, since a facelift addresses position and support, not skin quality. A tendency to keloid scarring, current blood thinners, or significant unmanaged medical conditions should be disclosed before travel. Because this surgery requires general anesthesia and an inpatient night, fitness for anesthesia is assessed carefully, and a thorough consultation confirms suitability before you commit to the trip.
sections.procedure_steps:
Care begins with a consultation, often started remotely with photos and completed in person, where the surgeon assesses your skin, the degree of sagging, and your facial structure, then agrees a plan: a SMAS or deep-plane lift, whether to add a neck lift, and whether fat grafting is needed to restore volume. Pre-operative health checks confirm fitness for general anesthesia.

The operation is performed under general anesthesia. Incisions are placed discreetly, typically along the hairline, around the ear, and sometimes under the chin for neck work, so scars are concealed in natural creases and the hairline. The surgeon lifts the skin, then works on the deeper SMAS layer, the supportive tissue under the skin, tightening and repositioning it. A deep-plane technique releases and lifts this layer more fully, while a SMAS approach tightens or folds it; the choice depends on your anatomy and goals.

If a neck lift is included, loose muscle bands and tissue under the chin are tightened. Fat grafting, when planned, harvests a small amount of your own fat and reinjects it to restore lost volume in the cheeks or other hollowed areas. Excess skin is then trimmed and redraped without excessive tension, and incisions are closed in layers.

Small drains are commonly placed to prevent fluid collecting under the skin, and a supportive dressing is applied. The operation typically takes several hours depending on whether neck lift and fat grafting are included. Most patients then spend about one night as an inpatient so the team can monitor recovery from anesthesia and check the drains.
sections.recovery_timeline:
The first days bring the most swelling and bruising, along with a tight, numb feeling across the cheeks and neck, which is normal as the deeper tissues settle. You spend about one night as an inpatient for monitoring, the dressing and drains are checked, and drains are usually removed within the first few days. Pain is generally described as moderate tightness and soreness rather than sharp pain, and is managed with prescribed medication.

Over the first week, swelling and bruising begin to ease, though they remain visible. Sutures are typically removed in stages around day 7 to 10 at follow-up visits, which aligns with the roughly 10-day stay. Most international patients are cleared to fly home toward the end of this period, once the surgeon confirms healing is progressing and any drains and key sutures are out.

From two to four weeks, the majority of bruising fades and you can return to light daily activity and gentle desk work, while still avoiding strenuous exercise, heavy lifting, and anything that raises blood pressure or risks a knock to the face. Numbness around the ears and cheeks can persist and gradually resolves over weeks to months.

The contour continues to refine as residual swelling settles over the following months, with the natural final result emerging later. KmedTour coordinators schedule your staged suture-removal visits before departure and relay later questions to the clinic, so your longer healing is supported once you are back home.
sections.cost_breakdown:
The stated range of $5,000 to $15,000 reflects the wide variation in facelift surgery, from a focused lower-face SMAS lift to an extended procedure combining a deep-plane lift, neck lift, and fat grafting. The quote typically covers the surgeon's fee, general anesthesia, the operating facility, the inpatient night, drains and dressings, standard pre-operative checks, and follow-up visits during your stay.

It generally excludes flights, accommodation, optional additions such as neck lift or fat grafting if not already included, and any unrelated treatments. Because the scope drives both cost and recovery, confirm in writing exactly what your personal quote covers before you travel.

Korea is competitive on price partly because of the high volume of facial surgery performed, which supports experienced surgeons and well-equipped facilities, and because facility, staffing, and overhead costs differ from those in the United States and parts of Europe. For international patients the aim is accredited, experienced care, including the inpatient stay and follow-up, at a total cost that, with travel factored in, often compares favorably with equivalent surgery closer to home. KmedTour provides an itemized quote so you can compare the same scope rather than headline numbers.
sections.why_korea:
Korea performs a high volume of facial cosmetic surgery, and surgeons there place particular emphasis on the deeper SMAS and deep-plane techniques that support natural, lasting results rather than tightening skin alone. This concentration of facial work builds broad experience across different face shapes and aging patterns, including patients traveling from Africa, the Middle East, and Asia.

Because a facelift involves general anesthesia, an inpatient night, and drains, the surrounding infrastructure matters. Korean clinics are typically well equipped, with established anesthesia, monitoring, sterilization, and aftercare protocols. The medical tourism sector is supported by the Ministry of Health and Welfare and promoted through KHIDI's Medical Korea program, and accreditation frameworks such as KOIHA exist to recognize facilities that meet defined standards for international patient care.

For patients coming from abroad, this combination of specialized surgical experience, proper inpatient facilities, and an established system for hosting international patients, including interpretation and coordinated scheduling, makes a more involved procedure feasible to plan around a single trip. KmedTour connects you with accredited hospitals and helps verify a facility's credentials so your decision rests on real standards rather than marketing.

faqs.q: Is a 10-day stay enough for a facelift, given it needs an inpatient night?
faqs.a: For most patients, yes. The roughly 10-day stay covers consultation, surgery, the inpatient night for monitoring, drain removal, and staged suture removal around day 7 to 10. The surgeon usually clears you to fly toward the end of that period once healing is on track. Swelling and some numbness persist at departure and continue settling over the following months.

faqs.q: When is it safe to fly home after a facelift?
faqs.a: Most patients are cleared to fly toward the end of the 10-day stay, after the inpatient night, drain removal, and key suture removal, once the surgeon confirms healing is progressing. Because this is a more involved operation under general anesthesia, flying too early is discouraged. Following in-flight advice, such as staying hydrated and moving your legs, supports safe travel after surgery.

faqs.q: Will I have language support throughout my stay?
faqs.a: Yes. KmedTour works with hospitals that provide interpretation for international patients, which matters more for a procedure involving general anesthesia and an inpatient night. Coordinators help with appointments, consent and anesthesia forms, the inpatient stay, and aftercare instructions in a language you understand. Tell us your preferred language in advance so an interpreter is arranged across your consultation, surgery, and follow-up.

faqs.q: What about follow-up or revision once I return home?
faqs.a: Aftercare continues after you fly home. KmedTour relays your later questions to the treating clinic and helps coordinate any follow-up, including revision discussion, which for a facelift is considered only after swelling and tissue settling are complete, often many months later. Keep your operative and anesthesia notes and aftercare instructions, and contact us if any concern arises during your longer healing.

Most patients describe tightness, soreness, and numbness across the cheeks and neck rather than sharp pain, and it is typically well controlled with prescribed medication. The first days, with peak swelling and the drains in place, are the most uncomfortable. The numb, tight sensation eases gradually over weeks as the deeper tissues settle and sensation returns.
